rule: V2 rule: when a time word starts the sentence, the verb stays in second position and the subject moves after it.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + complement.

translation: We go to the park today.
rule: Time before place: 'heute' (when) comes before 'in den Park' (where).
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + prepositional phrase.

translation: On Monday I have German class.
rule: V2 rule: 'am Montag' in first position pushes the subject after the verb.

translation: The children play in the garden.
rule: Basic SVO order: subject + verb + prepositional phrase.
Separable verbs: 'anfangen' splits — 'fängt' in V2 position, 'an' at the end.
V2 rule: 'jeden Tag' in first position pushes the subject after the verb.
W-questions: question word + verb + subject.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + adverb.

translation: On the weekend I drive to Hamburg.
rule: V2 rule: time expression first, then verb, then subject, then destination.
W-questions: question word + verb + subject.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + adverb + object.

translation: In the evening I read a book.
rule: V2 rule: 'abends' in first position pushes the subject after the verb.

translation: Maria and Tom go to school.
rule: Compound subjects: 'Maria und Tom' count as the subject, verb comes after.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + adverb + object.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + accusative object.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + prepositional phrase.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + prepositional phrase.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + accusative object.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + object.
Basic SVO order: subject + verb + accusative object.
V2 rule: 'heute' in first position, verb second, subject third.

translation: After breakfast I go to work.
rule: V2 rule: time expression first, verb second, subject third.

translation: In summer we drive to the sea.
rule: V2 rule: time expression 'im Sommer' in first position pushes the subject after the verb.

translation: On Friday we go to the cinema.
rule: V2 rule: 'am Freitag' in first position pushes the subject after the verb.
V2 rule: 'um 7 Uhr' in first position, verb second, subject third.
V2 rule: 'im Winter' in first position pushes the subject after the verb.
V2 rule: time expression first, verb second, subject third.
W-questions: question word + verb + subject.
W-questions: question word + verb + subject.
W-questions: question word + verb + subject.
W-questions: question word + verb + subject.
W-questions: question word + verb + subject + object.
W-questions: question word + verb + subject.

translation: How do I get to the train station?
rule: W-questions: question word + verb + subject + destination.
Yes/no questions: verb in first position, then subject.
Yes/no questions: verb in first position, then subject.
Yes/no questions: verb in first position, then subject.
Yes/no questions: verb in first position, then subject.
Yes/no questions: verb in first position, then subject.

translation: Are you going to Munich tomorrow?
rule: Yes/no questions: verb in first position, then subject.
Yes/no questions: verb in first position, then subject.
Separable verbs: 'aufstehen' splits — 'stehe' in V2 position, 'auf' at the end.
Separable verbs: 'anrufen' splits — 'ruft' in V2 position, 'an' at the end.
Separable verbs: 'einkaufen' splits — 'kaufen' in V2 position, 'ein' at the end.

translation: She watches TV in the evening.
rule: Separable verbs: 'fernsehen' splits — 'sieht' in V2 position, 'fern' at the end.
Separable verbs: 'anfangen' splits — 'fängt' in V2 position, 'an' at the end.
Separable verbs: 'aufräumen' splits — 'räume' in V2 position, 'auf' at the end.
Separable verbs: 'rausbringen' splits — 'bringt' in V2 position, 'raus' at the end.
Modal verbs: 'kann' in V2 position, infinitive 'schwimmen' at the end.
Modal verbs: 'musst' in V2 position, infinitive 'machen' at the end.

translation: We want to cook on Saturday.
rule: Modal verbs: 'wollen' in V2 position, infinitive 'kochen' at the end.

translation: He is not allowed to smoke here.
rule: Modal verbs: 'darf' in V2 position, infinitive 'rauchen' at the end. 'nicht' negates the permission.

translation: She would like to eat an ice cream.
rule: Modal verbs: 'möchte' in V2 position, infinitive 'essen' at the end.
Modal verbs: 'soll' in V2 position, infinitive 'anrufen' at the end.
Modal verbs in yes/no questions: modal verb first, infinitive at the end.

translation: We have to leave at 8 o'clock.
rule: Modal verbs: 'müssen' in V2 position, infinitive 'losfahren' at the end.
Negation with kein: 'keinen' negates the noun (masculine accusative).
Negation with nicht: 'nicht' comes at the end to negate the verb.
Negation with kein: 'keine' negates the feminine noun 'Zeit'.
Negation with nicht: 'nicht' comes before the possessive to negate it.
Negation with kein: 'kein' negates the neuter noun 'Auto'.
Negation with nicht: 'nicht' at the end negates the whole statement.
Negation with nicht: 'nicht' comes before the adjective it negates.

translation: I travel tomorrow by train to Cologne.
rule: TMP order: time ('morgen') before manner ('mit dem Zug') before place ('nach Köln').

translation: He walks to work every day.
rule: TMP order: time ('jeden Tag') before manner ('zu Fuß') before place ('zur Arbeit').

translation: We drive to Italy by car in August.
rule: TMP order: time ('im August') before manner ('mit dem Auto') before place ('nach Italien').

translation: She flies to London alone next week.
rule: TMP order: time ('nächste Woche') before manner ('allein') before place ('nach London').

translation: I like to go to the park in the evening.
rule: TMP order: time ('am Abend') before manner ('gern') before place ('in den Park').

translation: He comes home by bus at 3 o'clock.
rule: TMP order: time ('um 3 Uhr') before manner ('mit dem Bus') before place ('nach Hause').

translation: We cycle to the lake on Sunday.
rule: TMP order: time ('am Sonntag') before manner ('mit dem Fahrrad') before place ('zum See').
Compound subjects: 'Anna und ich' is the subject, verb follows in V2.

translation: My brother and my sister play tennis.
rule: Compound subjects: 'mein Bruder und meine Schwester' count as a plural subject.

translation: Lisa and Paul learn German together.
rule: Compound subjects: 'Lisa und Paul' is a compound subject taking a plural verb.

translation: Mom and Dad buy a new car.
rule: Compound subjects: 'Mama und Papa' is a compound subject taking a plural verb.

translation: The dog and the cat sleep in the living room.
rule: Compound subjects: 'der Hund und die Katze' count as a plural subject.

translation: Tim and I go to the swimming pool on Saturday.
rule: Compound subjects: 'Tim und ich' is the subject, verb follows in V2.
Dative verbs: 'gefallen' takes a dative object ('mir'). The thing liked is the subject.
Dative verbs: 'helfen' takes a dative object ('meiner Mutter').

translation: The phone belongs to my brother.
rule: Dative verbs: 'gehören' takes a dative object ('meinem Bruder').
Dative verbs: 'gefallen' takes a dative object ('meiner Schwester').
Dative verbs: 'danken' takes a dative object ('seinem Lehrer').
Dative verbs: 'antworten' takes a dative object ('dem Mann').
Dative verbs: 'helfen' takes a dative object ('den Kindern' — dative plural).
Perfekt: 'habe' (auxiliary) in V2 position, past participle 'gelernt' at the end.
Perfekt: 'hat' (auxiliary) in V2 position, past participle 'gebacken' at the end.
Perfekt: 'haben' in V2 position, past participle 'gespielt' at the end.
Perfekt with sein: movement verbs use 'ist' as auxiliary, past participle at the end.
Perfekt with sein: 'aufstehen' uses 'bin' as auxiliary, past participle at the end.
Perfekt: 'hat' in V2 position, past participle 'gelesen' at the end.
Perfekt with sein: movement verbs use 'sind' as auxiliary, past participle at the end.

translation: I learn German because I work in Germany.
rule: weil clause: after 'weil' the verb goes to the end of the clause.

translation: He says that he is coming tomorrow.
rule: dass clause: after 'dass' the verb goes to the end of the clause.

translation: I stay at home because it is raining.
rule: weil clause: after 'weil' the verb 'regnet' moves to the end of the clause.

translation: She knows that the test is difficult.
rule: dass clause: after 'dass' the verb 'ist' goes to the end of the clause.

translation: He is tired because he worked a lot.
rule: weil clause with Perfekt: after 'weil', the auxiliary 'hat' goes to the very end.

translation: I think that she is nice.
rule: dass clause: after 'dass' the verb 'ist' goes to the end of the clause.

translation: We don't eat because we are not hungry.
rule: weil clause: after 'weil' the verb 'haben' goes to the end of the clause.

translation: He hopes that the weather will be nice tomorrow.
rule: dass clause: after 'dass' the verb 'wird' goes to the end of the clause.
